card.indications: Treatment of erosive esophagitis and gastroesophageal reflux disease, maintenance of healing, and treatment of pathological hypersecretory conditions. IV therapy is used when oral treatment is not appropriate.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal-cell hydrogen-potassium ATPase, suppressing acid secretion.
card.dosing: Common adult dosing is 40 mg once daily for many GERD indications. Hypersecretory conditions may require higher or divided doses. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest appropriate duration.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow delayed-release tablets whole. Give oral suspension before a meal according to product directions. Reconstitute and administer IV formulations at the recommended concentration and rate.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to substituted benzimidazoles or product components. Concurrent rilpivirine-containing therapy is contraindicated.
card.warnings: Long-term use can increase risk of C. difficile diarrhea, fractures, hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, acute interstitial nephritis, lupus, and gastric polyps.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, vomiting, gas, and dizziness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute interstitial nephritis, severe hypomagnesemia, C. difficile colitis, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, lupus, hepatic injury, and blood dyscrasias.
card.interactions: May reduce absorption of drugs that require gastric acidity. High-dose methotrexate exposure may increase. Warfarin, digoxin, tacrolimus, and certain antiretroviral therapies may require monitoring.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom response, GI bleeding, magnesium with prolonged therapy, renal function, vitamin B12 with long-term use, bone risk, and persistent diarrhea or rash.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as directed and do not crush delayed-release tablets. Report persistent diarrhea, black stools, reduced urine, severe rash, muscle cramps, palpitations, or seizures.
card.nutrition: Take oral suspension before meals and tablets as directed. Avoid foods that worsen reflux and maintain adequate calcium and vitamin D if long-term therapy is needed.

card.indications: Treatment of major depressive disorder, panic disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, social anxiety disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, and selected menopausal vasomotor symptoms depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake in the CNS, increasing serotonergic neurotransmission.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication and formulation. Immediate-release therapy often begins at 10 to 20 mg once daily. Older adults and patients with renal or hepatic impairment generally require lower starting and maximum doses. Taper gradually when discontinuing.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily, usually in the morning, with or without food. Swallow controlled-release tablets whole. Shake suspension well. Avoid abrupt discontinuation.
card.contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use, use with thioridazine or pimozide, and hypersensitivity. Allow the required washout interval when switching to or from an MAO inhibitor.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: increased suicidal thoughts and behaviors in children, adolescents, and young adults. Risks include serotonin syndrome, severe withdrawal symptoms, hyponatremia, bleeding, mania, seizures, sexual dysfunction, and fetal or neonatal harm.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, drowsiness, insomnia, sweating, dry mouth, constipation, dizziness, decreased libido, delayed orgasm, and weight gain.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidal behavior, serotonin syndrome, severe hyponatremia, major bleeding, seizures, mania, angle-closure glaucoma, severe withdrawal, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors, linezolid, methylene blue, serotonergic drugs, and certain opioids increase serotonin-syndrome risk. NSAIDs, aspirin, anticoagulants, and antiplatelets increase bleeding. Paroxetine inhibits CYP2D6 and can alter many drugs, including tamoxifen.
card.monitoring: Monitor mood, anxiety, suicidal thinking, serotonin-syndrome symptoms, sodium in high-risk patients, bleeding, sexual effects, weight, sleep, and withdrawal during tapering.
card.patientTeaching: Take daily and do not stop suddenly. Report suicidal thoughts, severe agitation, fever, tremor, muscle rigidity, unusual bleeding, severe headache, confusion, or eye pain.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol and maintain normal fluid and sodium intake unless otherwise directed.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible serious infections, including selected streptococcal, pneumococcal, meningococcal, clostridial, spirochetal, and other infections. Exact salt and formulation determine administration details.
card.mechanism: Binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis, causing cell lysis in susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Dose is expressed in units and depends on infection, organism, age, renal function, and product. Serious infections may require several million units per day in divided doses or continuous infusion. Renal impairment requires adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: Verify the specific penicillin G salt, concentration, electrolyte content, and route. Reconstitute and infuse according to product directions. Obtain cultures before therapy when possible and complete the full prescribed course.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to penicillins or product components. Use caution with a history of severe cephalosporin or other beta-lactam allergy.
card.warnings: Can cause fatal anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, C. difficile diarrhea, seizures with high concentrations or renal impairment, electrolyte overload from potassium or sodium salts, blood dyscrasias, and nephritis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Injection-site pain, rash, nausea, diarrhea, fever, and mild electrolyte changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, seizures, hemolytic anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, interstitial nephritis, severe hyperkalemia or sodium overload, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: Probenecid increases penicillin concentrations. Bacteriostatic antibiotics may reduce activity in selected situations. Methotrexate clearance may decrease. Monitor potassium or sodium burden with other electrolyte-altering therapies.
card.monitoring: Monitor infection response, cultures, renal function, CBC during prolonged therapy, electrolytes, neurologic status with high doses, infusion site, allergy, and bowel pattern.
card.patientTeaching: Report rash, facial swelling, breathing difficulty, severe diarrhea, unusual bruising, or seizures immediately. Complete the entire course even if symptoms improve.
card.nutrition: No routine food restrictions because therapy is parenteral. Maintain hydration as ordered and account for sodium or potassium content in patients with restrictions.

card.indications: Short-term sedation or hypnosis, preanesthetic sedation, and emergency control of selected seizures or elevated intracranial pressure in monitored specialty settings.
card.mechanism: Enhances GABA-mediated inhibition at GABA-A receptors and, at higher doses, directly depresses neuronal activity, producing sedation, anesthesia, anticonvulsant effects, and respiratory depression.
card.dosing: Dose is highly indication-, age-, and setting-specific and must be titrated carefully. IV doses should be administered slowly. Lower doses are required in older, debilitated, or cardiorespiratory-compromised patients.
card.routeAdministration: Administer only where airway support, oxygen, suction, and resuscitation equipment are immediately available. Monitor continuously during IV use. Avoid intra-arterial injection and inspect the site for extravasation.
card.contraindications: Porphyria, severe respiratory disease with dyspnea or obstruction, marked hepatic impairment, hypersensitivity to barbiturates, and absence of appropriate monitoring for parenteral use.
card.warnings: Can cause profound respiratory and cardiovascular depression, coma, dependence, tolerance, withdrawal seizures, paradoxical excitement, and fatal overdose. Opioids, alcohol, and other CNS depressants greatly increase risk.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, confusion, impaired coordination, and injection-site reactions.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Apnea, respiratory arrest, severe hypotension, cardiovascular collapse, coma, severe tissue injury from extravasation or intra-arterial injection, dependence, and withdrawal seizures.
card.interactions: Opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, anesthetics, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory depression. Barbiturates induce hepatic enzymes and can reduce effects of anticoagulants, corticosteroids, doxycycline, and hormonal contraceptives.
card.monitoring: Monitor respiratory rate, oxygenation, ventilation, blood pressure, pulse, ECG when indicated, level of consciousness, airway patency, IV site, and signs of dependence or withdrawal.
card.patientTeaching: Do not drink alcohol, drive, or combine with sedatives unless directed. Long-term use should not be stopped abruptly. Seek emergency help for slowed breathing or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: Follow fasting instructions before procedural use. Resume food and fluids only after alertness and swallowing are safe. Avoid alcohol.

card.indications: Treatment of selected seizure disorders, including status epilepticus and neonatal seizures, and short-term sedation in selected settings.
card.mechanism: Enhances GABA-mediated inhibition and decreases excitatory neurotransmission, raising the seizure threshold while producing CNS and respiratory depression.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by indication, age, weight, serum concentration, seizure response, and organ function. Loading doses for status epilepticus require continuous monitoring. Maintenance therapy is adjusted gradually.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses at consistent times. Measure liquid accurately. Administer IV slowly through a secure line with continuous respiratory and cardiovascular monitoring. Do not stop chronic therapy abruptly.
card.contraindications: Porphyria, severe respiratory depression, marked hepatic impairment, prior barbiturate addiction, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause life-threatening respiratory depression, profound sedation, hypotension, dependence, tolerance, withdrawal seizures, suicidality, severe skin reactions, and fetal harm. It is a strong hepatic enzyme inducer.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, impaired coordination, cognitive slowing, nausea, irritability, and paradoxical excitement in children or older adults.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory arrest, severe hypotension, coma, status epilepticus after abrupt withdrawal, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, blood dyscrasias, hepatic injury, and dependence.
card.interactions: Opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory risk. Enzyme induction can reduce effectiveness of warfarin, hormonal contraceptives, corticosteroids, doxycycline, and many other drugs.
card.monitoring: Monitor seizure control, serum concentrations when indicated, respiratory status, blood pressure, sedation, cognition, CBC and liver function with long-term therapy, bone health, mood, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Avoid alcohol and other sedatives, use reliable backup contraception when advised, and report rash, breathing difficulty, severe sedation, mood changes, or worsening seizures.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain adequate calcium and vitamin D with long-term therapy and avoid alcohol.
